title: OPEC+ Approves Fourth Production Hike as Hormuz Crisis Enters 100th Day
subhead: OPEC+ agreed to raise output by 188,000 bpd for July—the fourth increase in four months—even as the Iran-US conflict keeps the Strait of Hormuz effectively closed and global energy markets strain under supply uncertainty.

sections.heading: OPEC+ Unwinding Cuts Despite Hormuz Closure
sections.body:
OPEC+ agreed on Sunday to raise its oil production targets by 188,000 barrels per day for July, marking the fourth consecutive monthly increase since the Strait of Hormuz closure began. The seven core members of the group—Saudi Arabia, Russia, Iraq, UAE, Kuwait, Kazakhstan, and Algeria—are gradually unwinding the 1.65 million bpd production cut they had maintained. Reuters reports that the decision comes despite the ongoing US-Iran conflict that has prevented several members from pumping more oil.

The move signals OPEC+'s calculation that removing Iranian supply from the market requires compensatory production, even as Gulf states navigate the physical risks of operating in a conflict zone. Brent crude has recovered from lows near $91 per barrel after initial expectations of a swift US-Iran agreement faded.
sections.whyItMatters: OPEC+ is essentially running a two-track strategy: compensating for Iranian supply loss while hoping diplomatic resolution eventually reopens Hormuz. The fourth consecutive hike shows they expect the crisis to persist.

sections.heading: Hormuz at 100 Days: The Economic Damage Spreads
sections.body:
As the US-Iran conflict approaches its 100th day, Anadolu Agency reports the closure of the Strait of Hormuz has triggered oil shocks, LNG supply constraints, and fertilizer shortages affecting global trade. The OECD warns of a potential $700 billion loss as global growth slows from 3.4% to 2.8%. The IEA forecasts warn of critically low oil stockpiles before peak summer demand if current draw rates continue.

The crisis has extended beyond oil to affect ammonia, urea, and fertilizer supplies—the 'petrochemical cells' of modern agriculture. Missouri corn farmers report being squeezed by fertilizer and diesel price spikes, while India's agriculture sector faces imported fertilizer costs rising 35-50%. The Strait of Hormuz carried roughly 20 million barrels per day of oil and significant LNG volumes; its effective closure has fundamentally disrupted global commodity flows.
sections.whyItMatters: The crisis has moved beyond oil prices into the real economy—affecting food production, industrial inputs, and sovereign finances. The fertilizer shock is particularly acute for emerging markets dependent on Gulf supplies.

sections.heading: The Rise of the 'Sahelocracies': Alternative Corridors Emerge
sections.body:
Analysis in Middle East Eye frames the Hormuz crisis as accelerating the emergence of 'Sahelocracies'—shore powers that bridge continents and offer passage outside contested chokepoints. Algeria is positioned as the archetype: a Mediterranean shoreline where continents meet, outside Hormuz's reach, with gas export infrastructure already serving Europe. Oman controls the southern shore of Hormuz yet has ports facing the open Indian Ocean, allowing it to maintain mediation credibility by refusing to weaponize the strait it co-owns.

The concept reflects a broader shift: states with coastal access to alternative routes are gaining strategic leverage as the old maritime order strains. Azerbaijan—landlocked but positioned at the Caspian-Caucasus interface—demonstrates the model can extend beyond the Arab world. The analysis argues we're seeing 'a nationalism of flows': states leaving resources locked in the ground rather than trusting markets through chokepoints.
sections.whyItMatters: Geography is being revalued. States that offer alternative transit routes—or neutrality in conflicts over chokepoints—are gaining bargaining power in a world where maritime corridors can be closed overnight.

sections.heading: Azerbaijan Emerges as Israel's Gas Buffer
sections.body:
MSN reports that Azerbaijan's SOCAR is providing backup gas supplies to Egypt and Jordan when Israeli gas exports are disrupted by the conflict. This positions Azerbaijan as an energy security buffer for regional allies of Israel, maintaining supply through alternative Mediterranean routes when Gulf shipments face interruption.

The arrangement demonstrates how the conflict is reshaping regional energy relationships: states with non-Hormuz-dependent export capacity—Azerbaijan via the Southern Gas Corridor, Algeria via Mediterranean pipelines—are seeing demand surge for their infrastructure as reliable alternatives to Gulf supplies.
sections.whyItMatters: The conflict is creating new energy relationships based on geography rather than ideology. Azerbaijan's role as an Israeli gas buffer would have been unthinkable a decade ago.

sections.heading: Putin's Nord Stream Gamble: Pressuring Germany on Gas
sections.body:
President Vladimir Putin urged Germany to resume Russian gas imports via Nord Stream on June 4, stating Russia was ready to supply Germany through the pipeline damaged by explosions in 2022. Reuters reported Putin saying the onus was on Berlin to decide. Federation Council Speaker Valentina Matviyenko, speaking at the St. Petersburg International Economic Forum, criticized Germany for refusing gas 'at 3-4 times higher prices' when Russian supplies remain available via the surviving Nord Stream string.

German far-right AfD official met with Putin's adviser and Gazprom's chief, calling for Nord Stream's reopening—highlighting how energy politics intersects with domestic political battles in Germany. The Druzhba pipeline continues supplying 165,000 bpd to Hungary and Slovakia, which maintained normal flows in May.
sections.whyItMatters: Russia is exploiting European energy anxiety to push for Nord Stream rehabilitation. Germany faces a political choice: maintain sanctions discipline on Russia while paying premium prices for alternative gas, or reopen a pathway that undermines European unity on Ukraine.

sections.heading: Japan Sets Conditions for Hormuz Mission Participation
sections.body:
Kyodo News reports that Japan has set three conditions for joining a potential international mission in the Strait of Hormuz. While the specific conditions weren't detailed in available reporting, Japan's government has historically required clear UN authorization, host nation consent, and limitations on combat roles before participating in overseas missions.

The deliberations reflect the dilemma facing Asian importers: 70% of Japan's oil imports transit Hormuz, yet direct involvement in a US-led mission risks Iranian retaliation and contradicts Japan's postwar security constraints. The conditions suggest Tokyo is seeking multilateral cover rather than bilateral alliance obligations.
sections.whyItMatters: Key Asian importers are reluctant to directly join US-led operations against Iran, even when their energy security depends on Hormuz passage. The conditions reflect the gap between alliance commitments and economic exposure.

sections.heading: IAEA Resolution on Iran: US Demands Transparency
sections.body:
Reuters reports the US is lobbying countries on the IAEA Board of Governors to back a draft resolution demanding Iran provide transparency on nuclear sites and uranium stocks. The resolution comes as the US-Iran conflict enters its 100th day with no diplomatic breakthrough in sight.

The IAEA push represents a parallel diplomatic track to the military confrontation—attempting to maintain international pressure on Iran's nuclear program while military operations continue. The timing suggests the US is pursuing a multi-pressure strategy: military strikes, economic sanctions, and nuclear transparency demands simultaneously.
sections.whyItMatters: Even amid active military conflict, nuclear transparency mechanisms remain relevant. The resolution reflects concern that Iran could accelerate nuclear activities while conventional military attention focuses elsewhere.

bottomLine: OPEC+'s fourth consecutive production hike confirms the group expects the Hormuz crisis to persist—replacing Iranian barrels while hoping for resolution. The 100-day conflict has moved beyond oil prices into fertilizer shortages, sovereign debt stress, and fundamental questions about maritime trade architecture. Alternative corridor states—Algeria, Oman, Azerbaijan—are gaining leverage as importers seek routes outside contested chokepoints. Russia is using European energy anxiety to push Nord Stream rehabilitation, while Asian importers like Japan seek multilateral cover for any Hormuz involvement. The old order of seamless maritime flows is giving way to a geography where transit routes, neutrality, and corridor access determine strategic value.
sources: Reuters, Middle East Eye, Anadolu Agency, Kyodo News, TASS, MSN, IEA, OECD, Fulton Sun, The Hans India
